raw_output: {"examples":[{"excerpt":"However, 2024 saw a dip in patenting activity. This drop was probably influenced by increasing overcapacity in the production of batteries, particularly in China."},{"excerpt":"However, despite this overall expansion, 2024 saw the first slight decline in patent publications since 2014."},{"excerpt":"However, this growth trend peaked in 2021 with 5,884 publications (and in 2020 for IPFs); subsequent annual activity has decreased by roughly 13%."}]}

examples.excerpt: However, 2024 saw a dip in patenting activity. This drop was probably influenced by increasing overcapacity in the production of batteries, particularly in China.
However, despite this overall expansion, 2024 saw the first slight decline in patent publications since 2014.
However, this growth trend peaked in 2021 with 5,884 publications (and in 2020 for IPFs); subsequent annual activity has decreased by roughly 13%.

raw_output: {"examples":[{"excerpt":"After the introduction of transformer models in 2017, both patent and scientific publications increased greatly, with scientific publications exploding after the 2022 release of ChatGPT."},{"excerpt":"Since 2017, China has published more patent families in this field every year than all other countries combined."},{"excerpt":"The share of GenAI patent publications among all AI patents has shown a slight uptick since 2017, from 4.2% to 6.1% in 2023."}]}

examples.excerpt: After the introduction of transformer models in 2017, both patent and scientific publications increased greatly, with scientific publications exploding after the 2022 release of ChatGPT.
Since 2017, China has published more patent families in this field every year than all other countries combined.
The share of GenAI patent publications among all AI patents has shown a slight uptick since 2017, from 4.2% to 6.1% in 2023.

raw_output: {"examples":[{"excerpt":"Patent activity has increased quite steadily since 2012 (compound annual growth rate, CAGR 2014–2019 = +8 percent) (Figure 7), indicating an increasing interest in the topic."},{"excerpt":"Patent activity has increased since 2012, with a CAGR of +11 percent between 2014 and 2019, which can be explained by the growing demand for electric cars (Figure 29)."},{"excerpt":"Patent activity shows an increasing interest since 2012, with a CAGR of +25 percent between 2014 and 2019 (Figure 49)."}]}

examples.excerpt: Patent activity has increased quite steadily since 2012 (compound annual growth rate, CAGR 2014–2019 = +8 percent) (Figure 7), indicating an increasing interest in the topic.
Patent activity has increased since 2012, with a CAGR of +11 percent between 2014 and 2019, which can be explained by the growing demand for electric cars (Figure 29).
Patent activity shows an increasing interest since 2012, with a CAGR of +25 percent between 2014 and 2019 (Figure 49).

raw_output: {"excerpt":"In terms of patent growth, patent families assigned to GANs show the strongest increase over the past decade. However, patent growth of GAN patent families has slowed down and GAN patent families have increased only moderately over the last three years."}

parsed_output.excerpt: In terms of patent growth, patent families assigned to GANs show the strongest increase over the past decade. However, patent growth of GAN patent families has slowed down and GAN patent families have increased only moderately over the last three years.

raw_output: {"excerpt":"The rise of GenAI over the past few years has been driven primarily by three factors: more powerful computers, the availability of large datasets as a source of training data, and improved AI/machine learning algorithms. Developments such as the transformer architecture in LLMs have significantly advanced GenAI. This has made it possible to develop complex applications in many different fields.\nThe technological advances in GenAI are reflected by the sharp increase in patenting activity.\nOver the past 10 years, the number of patent families in GenAI has grown from just only 733 in 2014 to more than 14,000 in 2023. Since the introduction of the transformer in 2017, the deep neural network architecture behind the Large Language Models that have become synonymous with GenAI, the number of GenAI patents has increased by over 800%. The number of scientific publications has increased even more over the same period, from just 116 in 2014 to more than 34,000 in 2023. Over 25% of all GenAI patents and over 45% of all GenAI scientific papers were published in 2023 alone."}

parsed_output.excerpt:
The rise of GenAI over the past few years has been driven primarily by three factors: more powerful computers, the availability of large datasets as a source of training data, and improved AI/machine learning algorithms. Developments such as the transformer architecture in LLMs have significantly advanced GenAI. This has made it possible to develop complex applications in many different fields.
The technological advances in GenAI are reflected by the sharp increase in patenting activity.
Over the past 10 years, the number of patent families in GenAI has grown from just only 733 in 2014 to more than 14,000 in 2023. Since the introduction of the transformer in 2017, the deep neural network architecture behind the Large Language Models that have become synonymous with GenAI, the number of GenAI patents has increased by over 800%. The number of scientific publications has increased even more over the same period, from just 116 in 2014 to more than 34,000 in 2023. Over 25% of all GenAI patents and over 45% of all GenAI scientific papers were published in 2023 alone.

raw_output: {"excerpt":"The number of patent families for speech/voice/music are the third and fourth largest modes in terms of patent families, with almost 13,500 families each over the same period."}

parsed_output.excerpt: The number of patent families for speech/voice/music are the third and fourth largest modes in terms of patent families, with almost 13,500 families each over the same period.

raw_output: {"excerpt":"Over the past 10 years, the number of patent families in GenAI has grown from just only 733 in 2014 to more than 14,000 in 2023."}

parsed_output.excerpt: Over the past 10 years, the number of patent families in GenAI has grown from just only 733 in 2014 to more than 14,000 in 2023.
The number of patent families for diffusion models increased from 18 in 2020 to 441 in 2023.

parsed_output.T: The average annual growth rate (compound annual growth rate) of patent families is calculated by dividing the count in the final year by the count in the initial year, taking the (t_final‑t_initial)th root of that ratio, and subtracting one; the result is expressed as a percentage indicating the typical yearly increase over the period.

raw_output: {"M":"A set of ordered pairs (y, c) where y is an integer year and c is a non‑negative integer count of distinct patent families for the given technology in that year; formally M = {(y_i, c_i) | i=1…n, y_i∈ℤ, c_i∈ℕ₀}.","T":"Each example provides a yearly time‑series of patent activity, indicating how the number of distinct patent families for a particular technology or region evolves over time."}

parsed_output.M: A set of ordered pairs (y, c) where y is an integer year and c is a non‑negative integer count of distinct patent families for the given technology in that year; formally M = {(y_i, c_i) | i=1…n, y_i∈ℤ, c_i∈ℕ₀}.
parsed_output.T: Each example provides a yearly time‑series of patent activity, indicating how the number of distinct patent families for a particular technology or region evolves over time.

parsed_output.M: For a series of years Y={y1,…,yn} with corresponding distinct patent‑family counts C={c1,…,cn}, define (i) yearly growth g_i = (c_i‑c_{i‑1})/c_{i‑1} for i>1, (ii) average annual growth A = (c_n/c_1)^{1/(n‑1)}‑1, (iii) peak year p = argmax_i c_i, and (iv) monotonic trend segments where g_i>0 (increase) or g_i<0 (decrease).
parsed_output.T: A natural‑language description that summarizes the observed trend in the yearly patent‑family counts – indicating periods of rapid increase, slowdown, peaks, or decline, naming notable years (e.g., the peak year or the start of a surge) and, when relevant, reporting the average annual growth rate.

{"excerpt":"Not only does China have the largest share of all patent families published globally but also is growing at the rate of 50% per year. However, India has an even higher growth rate of 56% per year even though its world share is still small. The Republic of Korea has also achieved high growth rates in their GenAI patent families since 2014. By contrast, patent families from Japan and the UK have only risen by a little more than 10% per year on average."}

parsed_output.excerpt: Not only does China have the largest share of all patent families published globally but also is growing at the rate of 50% per year. However, India has an even higher growth rate of 56% per year even though its world share is still small. The Republic of Korea has also achieved high growth rates in their GenAI patent families since 2014. By contrast, patent families from Japan and the UK have only risen by a little more than 10% per year on average.

raw_output: {"excerpt":"In terms of growth of patent families, image/video- and molecules/genes/proteins- based GenAI patent families increased the most over the last decade (an average annual growth rate of around 45% between 2014 and 2023). Molecules/genes/proteins GenAI patent families have also been the growth leaders in recent years. In contrast, the total number of GenAI patent families based on speech/sound/music has increased only slightly since 2021 (Figure 24)."}

parsed_output.excerpt: In terms of growth of patent families, image/video- and molecules/genes/proteins- based GenAI patent families increased the most over the last decade (an average annual growth rate of around 45% between 2014 and 2023). Molecules/genes/proteins GenAI patent families have also been the growth leaders in recent years. In contrast, the total number of GenAI patent families based on speech/sound/music has increased only slightly since 2021 (Figure 24).

parsed_output.M: A function f that maps an input paragraph to a set of 4‑tuples (entity, start_year, end_year, cagr_percent), where each tuple corresponds to an entity mentioned in the text together with the reported start and end years of the observation and the associated compound annual growth rate (CAGR) expressed as a percentage.
parsed_output.T: Extract every entity (e.g., technology, country, application area) for which the text reports a compound annual growth rate, capture the start and end years of the period referenced, and record the CAGR value; present these as rows in a table with columns entity, start_year, end_year, and cagr_percent.

parsed_output.M: M = { (entity, start_year, end_year, cagr_percent) | entity ∈ Entities extracted from the text, start_year = earliest year associated with entity, end_year = latest year associated with entity, cagr_percent = numeric CAGR value (in percent) explicitly stated for the entity in the text }
parsed_output.T: Extract from the given paragraph a list of entities together with the time interval (start and end year) over which their growth is described, and the corresponding compound annual growth rate (CAGR) percentage, and present these as rows in a compact CSV table with columns: entity, start_year, end_year, cagr_percent.
